meta.title: Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park (임진각 평화누리공원) | Paju, Gyeonggi-do · Korea's Iconic Peace Park by the DMZ
meta.description: The complete guide to Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park (임진각 평화누리공원): a peace park in Paju, Gyeonggi-do, near the Military Demarcation Line. Beyond Wind Hill, the Peace Bell and Freedom Bridge, the site preserves the lived memory of separated families, POW return, and the unresolved history of the DMZ.

schema.name: Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park
schema.description: A peace-themed public park in Paju, Gyeonggi-do, close to the Military Demarcation Line, combining memorial space, public green landscape, and border-history interpretation.

nonprofitBanner.text: This is an independent, non-profit guide compiled by travel enthusiasts to provide the most objective information about visiting Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park. It is free, and we are affiliated with no organization.
nonprofitBanner.dataSource: All historical background and park data on this site are cross-checked against public materials from the Korea Tourism Organization (VISITKOREA), the Gyeonggi Tourism Organization and Paju City, with no commercial recommendations.

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.text: So that every traveler visiting Paju can experience this green space built for peace and reunification, we have organized scattered official information into this guide — only to give you the purest open field, pinwheels and moments of reflection.

hero.title: Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park (임진각 평화누리공원)
hero.subtitle: A vast green peace park in Paju, Gyeonggi-do, right beside the Military Demarcation Line, built to pray for peace and reunification of the Korean Peninsula. Its iconic Wind Hill — thousands of colorful pinwheels — along with the Peace Bell, Freedom Bridge and lawns displaying war relics and peace art installations, form a cultural landmark for looking back at history and offering hopes for peace.

sunsetPlanner.title: Based on today's sunset, we suggest arriving about 60 minutes earlier to catch both the softest diffuse light and the blue-hour sky — ideal for photographing the colorful pinwheels of Wind Hill and the silhouette of the Peace Bell.
sunsetPlanner.note: The park is open and unobstructed; light is warmest from afternoon to dusk. On peak weekends and holidays, allow extra time to avoid the crowds.
sunsetPlanner.seaGlare: Open-ground glare note: Imjingak sits on flat open fields by the Imjin River. Afternoon to dusk gives the best front light, with the clearest detail on the pinwheels and Peace Bell; early-morning river mist is best for quiet, ethereal wide shots.

quickFactsDashboard.title: Imjingak at a Glance · Data Board
quickFactsDashboard.subtitle: Read this riverside memorial landscape through a set of numbers that carry more historical weight than a typical park summary.

items.label: Founded
items.note: Imjingak was first created for separated families. It was not conceived as a leisure park, but as a place where people unable to return home could face north and hold on to memory.

items.label: Wind Hill
items.note: The pinwheels of Wind Hill are often read as an allusion to the traditional phrase 'three thousand ri of Korean land', turning peace into a visible public landscape.

items.label: Jangdan Station steam locomotive
items.note: The ruined locomotive known as the 'Iron Horse' remains one of the clearest physical witnesses of wartime destruction, now preserved as Registered Cultural Heritage No. 78.

items.label: Freedom Bridge
items.note: After the 1953 armistice, about 12,773 South Korean and UN prisoners of war returned south through this crossing, giving the bridge its enduring moral force.

items.label: Mangbaedan Altar
items.note: Mangbaedan remains one of the most meaningful places where separated families bow toward their northern hometowns during major holidays.

items.label: Coordinates
items.note: At 148-40 Imjingak-ro, Munsan-eup, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do, the park's location within the southern DMZ frontier is itself part of its historical meaning.

quickFactsDashboard.distanceSubtitle: Converting abstract border tension into kilometers helps visitors grasp why Imjingak feels different from an ordinary suburban park.

distanceMetrics.label: Distance to the Military Demarcation Line
distanceMetrics.note: This short straight-line distance explains much of the psychological pressure and symbolic charge that still surrounds the site.

distanceMetrics.label: Distance to Kaesong Industrial Complex
distanceMetrics.note: The proximity to Kaesong shows how closely everyday geography and inter-Korean history continue to overlap in this region.

distanceMetrics.label: Distance to Seoul City Hall
distanceMetrics.note: Imjingak is not far from the capital, which is precisely why it remains such an accessible public gateway to questions of division, peace, and reunification.

hiddenRules.title: Safety Tips Near the Military Zone
hiddenRules.subtitle: Although Imjingak is an open park, it sits right next to the Demilitarized Zone (DMZ) and the Military Demarcation Line. Think about IDs, controls and safety, and the experience upgrades from 'rushing check-ins' to 'relaxed, safe play'.

highlights.label: Bring ID
highlights.note: Deep-DMZ experiences (such as the Peace Gondola or Peace Train Station) may require on-site passport registration. Bring valid ID when you go.

highlights.label: Controlled areas
highlights.note: The park is next to the Military Demarcation Line. Follow signs and fences strictly; do not enter closed or military-controlled areas or climb railings.

highlights.label: Airspace limits
highlights.note: Near the military zone, drones are usually banned over the park and DMZ. Respect local airspace and filming restrictions.

panels.q: Do I need a passport to enter Imjingak?
panels.a: The public park area is free to enter without a passport. But deep-DMZ experiences such as the Peace Gondola or Peace Train Station that cross the Imjin River into the DMZ usually require on-site passport registration. Bring it along.
items: The public green area is open 24 hours, anytime.
Deep-DMZ experiences follow official same-day rules; check ahead.

panels.q: Why be careful about military control and fences?
panels.a: Imjingak is next to the Military Demarcation Line, and some areas are military-controlled. Even when it looks calm, you may be near sensitive ground. Follow on-site guidance, do not enter closed zones — both for your safety and to respect the memorial meaning of this land.

panels.q: Can I light a fire or set off fireworks in the park?
panels.a: No. Near the military zone, open fires and private fireworks are strictly prohibited in the public green space. Follow on-site staff and stay in designated areas.

complianceGuide.title: Imjingak · Park Etiquette & Public Environment Guide
complianceGuide.subtitle: This is both a visitor's park and a memorial where Koreans place their hopes for peace and reunification. Following these rules is respect for history and for others.

rules.title: Take your trash with you
rules.text: Park bins are limited and the open field is windy, scattering litter. Bring a small bag and take everything away, especially cigarette butts, plastics and food waste.

rules.title: Enjoy quietly
rules.text: Many come specifically to offer hopes for peace and separated families. Lower your voice and don't blast music, leaving the calm to the field and the bell.

rules.title: No smoking & fire-free
rules.text: Near the military zone, the whole area is smoke-free and open flames are banned; be especially careful with fire on lawns and wooden facilities.

rules.title: Respect the memorial
rules.text: The Peace Bell, Freedom Bridge and memorial installations carry real history. Do not climb, carve or behave inappropriately; keep the memorial space solemn.

higashiyamaRoute.title: Custom Itinerary: Imjingak Half-Day Loop
higashiyamaRoute.subtitle: Not just 'who it suits', but a walkable half-day route. Centered on Wind Hill, it links the Peace Bell, Freedom Bridge and the park's memorial hall.

steps.title: [Start] Wind Hill & the pinwheel sea
steps.text: First be surrounded by thousands of colorful pinwheels on the open Wind Hill, photograph and unwind in the Imjin River breeze, leaving the soft morning or dusk light for this place of peace.
tips: Wind Hill is open and unobstructed; morning and dusk light are best.

steps.title: [Main] Peace Bell & wishes
steps.text: Walk along the path to the Peace Bell, ring it or stand in silent wish, and understand Imjingak's original intent as a symbol of peace and reunification.
tips: The bell area is solemn; lower your voice and avoid running.

steps.title: [Extend] Freedom Bridge & history trail
steps.text: Walk to Freedom Bridge and learn how about 13,000 POWs returned south in 1953, weaving war memory and peace wishes into one experience.
tips: The bridge is a memorial facility; follow the designated path and do not climb railings.

steps.title: [Supply] Lawn & art installations
steps.text: Stroll and picnic (take your trash) on the lawn displaying war relics and peace art installations, feeling history and contemporary art together.
tips: The open lawn suits slow family visits.

steps.title: [End] Peace Gondola or return
steps.text: If time allows and IDs are ready, experience the Peace Gondola crossing the Imjin River into the DMZ; otherwise take a bus / taxi back, completing a 'pinwheels + bell + history' half-day package.
tips: The gondola requires passport registration; confirm same-day operation ahead.

higashiyamaRoute.disclaimer: The route emphasizes a closed loop that 'works even if you just follow it'; if you only want the pinwheels, keep the first two segments and treat the gondola and hall as flexible add-ons.

intro.title: Getting to Know Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park
intro.description: Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park (임진각 평화누리공원) lies in Munsan-eup, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do, by the Imjin River and just south of the Military Demarcation Line (DMZ) — a vast green peace park built to pray for peace and reunification of the Korean Peninsula. Established in 1972 to hold the longings of separated families for relatives in the North, it is now both a relaxing public space for citizens and an important cultural landmark to feel the history of division and the Korean War up close. Its signature Wind Hill — thousands of colorful pinwheels — is a perfect photo spot; the Peace Bell, Freedom Bridge, and lawns displaying war relics and peace art installations together form an open space for looking back at history and offering hopes for peace.

visitGuide.title: Visit Guide
visitGuide.description: Imjingak is a free, open public green space with no gate and no reservation; the public area is accessible 24 hours. The ideal approach is usually not 'wander by day' but reverse-planning around light: come at dawn or dusk for the best light on Wind Hill, and prefer weekdays for quiet reflection. Facilities are restrained and commercial feel is low — which is exactly its charm.
items: Best at dawn or dusk: the Imjin River light is softest then, and pinwheels with the Peace Bell frame best.
Best viewpoints are the high point of Wind Hill and the Peace Bell plaza.
About a 10–15 min walk from Imjingang Station to the main sights.
Almost no commercial supply in the park; water, restrooms and breaks are better at the entrance or nearby.

alsoKnownAs.title: Highlights
items: Wind Hill of thousands of colorful pinwheels — Imjingak's most photogenic spot
The Peace Bell you can ring to pray for peace and reunification
Freedom Bridge (1953 POW return) carrying real war and reunion memory
Open lawn displaying war relics and peace art — history meets art
Next to the DMZ, linkable with the Peace Gondola and Third Infiltration Tunnel

transportSection.title: Transportation & Getting Here
transportSection.subtitle: Combine arriving in Paju, city transfers, walking/cycling, parking and charging into one clear structure for planning your Imjingak trip.
transportSection.overviewText: Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park is at 148-40 Imjingak-ro, Munsan-eup, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do, next to the DMZ and a representative peace park of Paju. The easiest long-distance option is the Gyeongui-Jungang Line from Seoul to Imjingang Station, about 1 hour; once in the park, Imjingang Station is about a 10–15 min walk to the main sights. Imjingak is an open public green space, reachable on foot from the station.
transportSection.overviewText2: Imjingak is open parkland with no gate. Plan transport, parking and walking together — especially with seniors, children or luggage, parking at a public lot then walking in saves a lot of hassle.
arrivalItems: Imjingak is in Munsan-eup, Paju; about a 10–15 min walk from Imjingang Station to the main sights.
Several public parking lots surround the park; they fill fast on peak weekends — arrive early or prefer transit.
Crowds are higher on weekends and holidays; strongly prefer public transit or off-peak visits. Bring your passport for DMZ experiences.

modes.label: Train (Gyeongui-Jungang Line · Imjingang Stn)
modes.title: Train direct to Imjingang Station
modes.description: Easiest for most travelers; from Seoul take the Gyeongui-Jungang Line to Imjingang Station — the classic way to Imjingak.
details: From Seoul take the Gyeongui-Jungang Line to Imjingang Station (Imjingang Station), about 1 hour.
Imjingang is a dedicated Imjingak station; a 10–15 min walk from the platform to the park.
Use T-money / transport card for easy bus and taxi connections.

steps.t: Take the Gyeongui-Jungang Line to Imjingang Station.
Walk along park signs about 10–15 min.
Reach Wind Hill and the Peace Bell.

modes.label: Public transit (intercity bus / transfer)
modes.title: Seoul etc. transfer to Paju
modes.description: Convenient for most; take an express / intercity bus to Paju or Munsan, then transfer to a city bus or taxi to Imjingak.
details: From Seoul take a bus to Paju / Munsan Intercity Terminal, about 1–1.5 hours.
After arriving, transfer to a city bus or taxi to Imjingak.
Buses accept T-money; use a map app for real-time arrivals.

steps.t: Take express / intercity bus to Paju or Munsan.
Transfer to city bus or taxi to Imjingak.
Walk from the entrance into the park.

modes.label: Imjingang Station (walk 10–15 min)
modes.title: Nearest station, hidden option
modes.description: Imjingang Station is the closest station to Imjingak; a 10–15 min walk reaches Wind Hill — best for light packers who want to see it first.
details: Imjingang is a small dedicated station; some trains stop, check schedules ahead.
From the platform, follow park signs about 10–15 min to the main sights.
Dawn and weekdays are best to avoid Seoul-side transfer crowds.

steps.t: Alight at Imjingang Station by train.
Walk along park signs about 10–15 min.
Reach Wind Hill and the Peace Bell.

modes.label: Driving (parking / charging)
modes.title: Via highway · nearby parking
modes.description: Good with children, seniors, lots of luggage or a Gyeonggi tour; several public lots surround the park.
details: Set destination to '임진각 평화누리공원' or address '경기도 파주시 문산읍 임진각로 148-40'.
Park public lots are free or low-cost, but fill on peak weekends.
Some public lots have EV chargers; open field is windy, mind crosswinds and parking.

steps.t: Park in a public / paid lot, walk in.
Avoid the 10:00–18:00 peak to save parking time.

modes.label: Taxi / ride-hail
modes.title: Door-to-door
modes.description: Easiest with luggage, children/seniors or early / late arrivals.
details: Taxi from Munsan or Paju city to Imjingak about 15–25 min, meter based.
Walk from the park entrance into the park.
Taxis queue on peak days; reserve via a local app (e.g. Kakao T).

steps.t: Call a car via Kakao T etc.
Alight at the park entrance, walk in.

modes.label: Cycling / walking
modes.title: Park greenway & field stroll
modes.description: The most relaxing way to feel the Imjin River fields and pinwheels.
details: The park is flat; walk to link Wind Hill, Peace Bell and Freedom Bridge.
Park bikes at the racks; no riding on lawns or memorial areas.
About a 10–15 min walk from Imjingang Station to Wind Hill, open fields throughout.

steps.t: Stroll the river or rent a public bike near the station / park.
Walk the greenway toward Wind Hill.
Lock the bike at the bell plaza, walk to visit.

modes.label: Walking (neighborhood roam)
modes.title: From parking to park
modes.description: If already in Munsan or near the park, walking is the natural way to observe the field and memorial space.
details: About a 3–10 min walk from surrounding public lots to the main sights.
Pass fields, green space and memorial installations along the way.
Lawns have slight slopes; wear comfortable shoes and watch children.

parking.title: Parking & Charging Overview
parking.note: Several public parking lots surround Imjingak; below are the nearest main options. Rates and availability vary by season and time — follow on-site signs.

options.name: Imjingak public parking
options.price: Public free or low-cost, fills in peak

options.name: Peace Gondola parking
options.price: Park annex parking, more spaces but tight in peak

options.name: Surrounding street parking
options.price: Roadside / small lots, few spaces, easier off-peak

options.name: Munsan city parking
options.price: City / mall parking, tight on holidays

parking.disclaimer: Park roads are extremely congested on holidays and peak season; do not block bus and fire lanes. EV chargers are common at park public lots and the Peace Gondola lot; rates and limits may change — check posted signs.

driverFaq.a: Several public lots surround the park, such as Imjingak public parking and Peace Gondola parking, within walking distance. They fill fast on peak weekends — arrive early or park further out and walk the last stretch.
Imjingak public parking is about 100–300 m away, the closest to Wind Hill; Peace Gondola parking is about 300–500 m, more spaces but tight in peak.
Very little. Park roads are narrow and congested on holidays; use proper lots and public transit, no long roadside parking.
Unless parking is essential, driving is not advised. Weekends and peak season are very congested; walking or transit is smoother. If driving, park at a surrounding lot then walk in.
Strongly. Take the Gyeongui-Jungang Line to Imjingang Station, then walk about 10–15 min to the park. Address: 148-40 Imjingak-ro, Munsan-eup, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea.

driverFaq.q: What is the best way to get here?
driverFaq.a: For stability and ease, transit is still best: Gyeongui-Jungang Line to Imjingang Station, walk in. If you must drive, treat parking and transfer as part of the trip, not 'park at the door'.

localTips: Dawn and dusk remain the golden windows, but set your baseline as 'arrive 60 min before sunset' — best for pinwheels and the Peace Bell.
Crowds are high on weekends and summer break; allow buffer and off-peak time with kids or gear.
Imjingak pairs best with the Peace Gondola, Third Tunnel and Dora Observatory; a single stop underestimates its historical value.

bestTime.title: Best time to arrive
bestTime.text: Imjingak is accessible all day, but the photo ceiling is the dawn and dusk light window. Arrive about 60 min before sunset; if weather is poor for photos, shift focus to the Peace Gondola, the park memorial hall or nearby Heyri Art Village.

officialManagement.title: About This Green Space
officialManagement.text: Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park lies in Munsan-eup, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do, by the Imjin River, a peace memorial green space grown from former open fields along the Imjin River, maintained as public space by Paju City and Gyeonggi-do. It has long been an open place for citizens' strolls, travelers' gazing at the demarcation line and shared wishes for peace, and is one of Paju's city cards as the 'City of Peace and Memory'.

humanities.title: Humanities & Peace Theme Research
humanities.subtitle: If Imjingak is read only as a photogenic park of pinwheels and open lawns, its real core is lost. What gives this place weight is injury, separation, waiting, and an unquiet desire for freedom.

knowledge.title: Imjingak's History & Origins
knowledge.storyDeckSubtitle: Read the Iron Horse, Mangbaedan, Freedom Bridge, and Wind Hill together, and Imjingak becomes less a collection of viewpoints than a walkable archive of border memory.

storyBoxes.eyebrow: Historic Relic · Jangdan Station Steam Locomotive
storyBoxes.title: The 'Iron Horse' with 1,020 bullet holes
storyBoxes.intro: On 31 December 1950, engineer Han Jun-ki drove a Mater 2-10 steam locomotive loaded with military supplies southward in retreat. At Jangdan Station, he discovered that the track ahead had been destroyed and the route behind was cut off.
items: To prevent the locomotive and its cargo from being captured, Allied forces destroyed and detonated it. The steel wreck remained in the grasslands of the DMZ for decades.
In 2004, the South Korean government recovered the locomotive from inside the DMZ and transferred it to Imjingak for chemical preservation and public display.
The phrase painted on its side, 'The Iron Horse Wants to Run Again', turns national division into a single frozen image: a machine built for movement, forced into permanent stillness.
storyBoxes.closing: It is not simply a war relic, but one of the clearest witnesses to a history that never fully moved on.

storyBoxes.title: Silent grief at Mangbaedan
storyBoxes.intro: Built in 1985, Mangbaedan is one of the closest publicly accessible ritual sites from which ordinary South Koreans can face north without special permission.
items: For decades, separated families have gathered here during Seollal and Chuseok, setting out memorial offerings and bowing toward hometowns they can no longer reach.
For many visitors, Mangbaedan is not symbolic in an abstract sense. It is as close as they will ever come to home again.
The granite reliefs behind the altar, depicting scenes of the northern provinces, function less as decoration than as a stone map of unfinished return.
storyBoxes.closing: Its emotional gravity lies not in the structure itself, but in a waiting that history never allowed to end.

storyBoxes.title: The moment of throwing off prison clothes
storyBoxes.intro: After the 1953 Korean War Armistice, a temporary one-way wooden bridge about 83 meters long was erected beside the destroyed railway crossing for prisoner exchange.
items: Around 12,773 South Korean and UN prisoners of war crossed here back to the South. 'Freedom Bridge' is not a poetic nickname but the record of a specific historical passage.
Accounts describe returning POWs removing the prison uniforms issued in northern camps, throwing them into the Imjin River, and shouting in celebration as they crossed south.
That is why the bridge remains more than a photo stop: it condenses bodily release, political symbolism, and emotional return into one memorial scene.
storyBoxes.closing: To stand before it is to confront a crossing that was once felt with the full weight of the body.

storyBoxes.title: 3,000 pinwheels and a borderless wind
storyBoxes.intro: Wind Hill's signature installation of 3,000 pinwheels, created by artist Choi Pyung-gon for the 2005 World Peace Festival, is often interpreted as an allusion to the 'three thousand ri' of the Korean Peninsula.
items: The pinwheels matter not only because they are beautiful, but because they translate a heavy historical site into a public image of peace that can be immediately understood.
The idea that 'wind has no border' gives the installation its deeper force. In a frontier landscape shaped by fences and control lines, the wind becomes a gentle refusal of division.
When thousands of pinwheels rotate at once in the river wind, the effect is not festival spectacle alone, but a visible structure of collective wishing.
storyBoxes.closing: Wind Hill is therefore both the color of Imjingak and one of its softest historical languages.

sections.title: Founding intent: built for separated families
sections.content: Imjingak was established in 1972 not as an entertainment venue, but as a public place where families separated by war could face north and hold on to memory. That origin still matters: even today's lawns, walkways, and pinwheels rest on an emotional ground shaped by loss, distance, and suspended return.

sections.title: The name 'Imjingak': a pavilion by the Imjin River
sections.content: 'Imjin' comes from the Imjin River, while 'gak' refers to a pavilion-like memorial structure by the water. The name quietly defines the site as both a real riverside geography and a platform of looking northward, where landscape and national memory overlap.

sections.title: Next to the DMZ: south of the line
sections.content: Imjingak lies close to the Military Demarcation Line, making it one of the few places where the politics of division remain spatially legible to the public. Here, the border is not an abstraction found only in books; it is read through riverbanks, fences, bridges, and the short distance separating everyday South Korea from the unresolved frontier.

sections.title: Wind Hill: peace made visible
sections.content: Wind Hill is the most photographed part of Imjingak, yet its importance lies beyond imageability. By making wind visible through rotation, the pinwheels turn a large political wish into an everyday, legible, public landscape. They soften the site without emptying it of historical seriousness.

sections.title: Peace Bell: ring for wishes
sections.content: The Peace Bell derives its force less from scale than from ritual. To ring it is to place a personal wish inside a larger historical field: one visitor may pray for a peaceful journey, another for reunification, another simply that war not return. In that sense, the bell gathers private emotion into a shared civic act.

sections.title: Freedom Bridge: where POWs returned
sections.content: Freedom Bridge matters because 'freedom' here was once experienced physically, step by step. About 12,773 South Korean and UN prisoners of war crossed back to the South after the armistice. The bridge remains powerful because it records not an abstract principle, but a bodily return from captivity.

sections.title: National Memorial Museum of the Abductees
sections.content: The National Memorial Museum of the Abductees in the Korean War, located within the wider Imjingak area, extends the site's historical frame beyond front lines alone. Its archives and exhibitions remind visitors that the human cost of division also includes abduction, disappearance, family rupture, and grief carried across generations.

sections.title: Peace Gondola: across the Imjin River
sections.content: The Peace Gondola changes the experience of Imjingak from distant observation to controlled approach. By crossing the Imjin River into a more tightly managed frontier zone, it makes clear how tourism, security, geography, and memory are layered together in this border landscape.

didYouKnow.title: Did you know?
didYouKnow.text: What makes Imjingak unusual is not any single monument, but the way separated-family memory, war damage, ritual space, public art, and open green landscape occupy the same ground. Every quiet walk here takes place inside an unfinished history.

sitePlaques.title: Reading the On-Site Signs
sitePlaques.subtitle: What's worth reading slowly on site is often not the photo badge, but the official signs explaining 'why this land is here'.
sitePlaques.intro: The readings below are based on Paju City and Gyeonggi's Imjingak introductions, peace-theme notes and DMZ guides, turning information visible on site but not always read closely into understandable English.

items.title: Imjingak & separated families
items.body: These signs give the key background: founded in 1972 to hold separated families' longings for the North, and Imjingak's meaning as the closest public memorial to the North. Reading the signs is lesson one in using this memorial green space.

items.title: Wind Hill & wishes
items.body: The signs stress Imjingak's status as a peace memorial and remind visitors that half the beauty of this field is the pinwheels, half is the real history underfoot. They explain clearly 'why so many pinwheels'.

items.title: Historic site of POW return
items.body: The guide explains 'why this bridge is called Freedom'. In 1953 about 13,000 POWs returned south across it; the bridge is preserved as a memorial. Seeing it with Wind Hill, Imjingak's design logic is clear: light hope and heavy history coexist.

items.title: Peace Gondola across the Imjin
items.location: In front of the gondola station
items.body: Erected by Paju City, marking the Peace Gondola as the official experience crossing the Imjin River into the DMZ. It reminds every visitor: this green space connects to the most sensitive yet most worth-gazing border of the Korean Peninsula.

gallery.title: Photo Gallery
gallery.subtitle: From the colorful pinwheels of Wind Hill, the Peace Bell silhouette and Freedom Bridge to the open Imjin River fields — the visual beauty of Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park.

reviews.title: Visitor Reviews
reviews.declaration: Visitor feedback is available on Google Maps (external link).
reviews.featuredSubtitle: A few short lines that capture Imjingak's character.

featured.text: Thousands of colorful pinwheels turning by the Imjin River — on one side light hope, on the other heavy history. So thoughtful.
A free, open peace park not far from Seoul yet calming — Paju's gentlest corner.
With kids slowly along the lawn, Wind Hill surprised the little ones and was easy for seniors too.

items.name: Minjun
items.text: Visited at dawn; the pinwheels are super photogenic in backlight, and the moment ringing the Peace Bell was silent — strongly recommend dusk, best light.

items.name: Seoyeon
items.text: Wind Hill's composition is striking; the gentle entrance is family-friendly, riverside wind is strong so bring a jacket.

items.name: Jihoon
items.text: Worth it as a free public green space; weekends get crowded, weekdays or early morning are more comfortable.

items.name: Ha-eun
items.text: About a 10 min walk from Imjingang Station to Wind Hill; the open field along the way is pleasant, good for a half-day stroll.

mapSection.title: How to Get Here
mapSection.subtitle: 148-40 Imjingak-ro, Munsan-eup, Paju-si, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea (Plus Code: VPRV+V6)

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.text: So that every traveler visiting Paju can experience this green space built for peace and reunification, we have organized scattered official information into this guide — only to give you the purest open field, pinwheels and moments of reflection.

footer.officialResourcesIntro: This page links only to Korean government or semi-official public institutions, avoiding commercial referral patterns and keeping the reference system clear, restrained, and trustworthy.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.subtitle: Practical information about Imjingak Pyeonghwa Nuri Park's facilities, history and visit planning.

items.label: Parking
items.value: Imjingak public lot / Peace Gondola lot

items.label: Fuel / EV charge
items.value: EV chargers at nearby public lots

items.q: Is there parking, and what does it cost?
items.a: Several public lots surround the park, such as Imjingak public parking and Peace Gondola parking, within walking distance. Mostly free or low-cost; they fill fast on peak weekends — arrive early or prefer transit.
The lawn and main paths are flat with accessible ramps; wheelchairs and strollers can reach Wind Hill and the Peace Bell fairly easily. Soft lawn still resists wheels, so use hard surfaces.

items.q: Are there restrooms or food on site?
items.a: Restrooms are at the park entrance and public areas; dining and convenience stores cluster in Munsan city — stock up on water and food at the entrance or in the city before entering.

items.q: Is there a gas station or EV charging nearby?
items.a: EV chargers are at the park-side public lots and Peace Gondola parking; conventional gas stations line Munsan and Paju city, handy for drivers to refuel on the way in.

items.q: What does 'Imjingak' have to do with geography?
items.a: 'Imjin' comes from the Imjin River, and 'gak' means a riverside pavilion-style memorial. The park sits just south of the DMZ — one of the closest points where ordinary Koreans can gaze toward the North. The name hides both the river's geography and a character that faces north and keeps silent watch.

items.q: What is its special natural and human value?
items.a: Imjingak is not a themed park, but a public field where citizens turned a stretch of Imjin River bank into a memorial landmark holding together peace wishes, separated-families memory, Freedom Bridge history and pinwheel art. Wind Hill, the Peace Bell and Freedom Bridge form a low-impact, high-empathy design and one of Paju's 'peace and memory' city cards.

Imjingak is an open public green space, free long-term, the public area is accessible 24 hours, no gate, no ticket or reservation — visit anytime (respect the memorial meaning, avoid late-night noise).

items.q: How long does a visit take?
items.a: A relaxed visit is about 1–2 hours (including Wind Hill and photo stops); if you add the Peace Gondola, memorial hall or Third Tunnel, reserve a half day.

items.q: Can I still go in bad weather?
items.a: Yes — it is an open green space, accessible in any weather. But during typhoons, high wind or thunderstorms mind field wind protection and lightning; wear non-slip shoes and watch weather; avoid riverside steps and open high points in bad weather.

items.q: What else is worth visiting nearby?
items.a: From Imjingak you can link the Paju Imjingak Peace Gondola (across the Imjin River into the DMZ), the Third Infiltration Tunnel (제3땅굴), Dora Observatory (도라전망대), Heyri Art Village (헤이리 예술마을) and Paju Book City, forming a 'pinwheels – history – art' half-day route.

attractions.name: Paju Imjingak Peace Gondola (across Imjin River into DMZ)
Third Infiltration Tunnel (제3땅굴 · DMZ battlefield relic)

scienceArt.title: Peace & History: Wind, Bell and Border
scienceArt.subtitle: Dig one layer below the surface 'good looks' to find what is truly rare here: it is at once a memorial, a piece of national memory, and an open-air peace classroom.

scienceMuseum.name: Imjin River & the Demarcation Line
scienceMuseum.role: A public green space built south of the border
scienceMuseum.highlight: The hardest part of Imjingak is both visible and invisible. Visible are the pinwheels and Peace Bell; invisible is the Military Demarcation Line it borders and the unfinished division history. Visitors see scenery; historians see a memory system still running.
points: Core: separated-family memory, peace wishes and border geography light up this green space.
Key: public memorial transformed into low-impact, high-empathy space.
Meaning: it upgrades 'border wilderness' into a public peace model.

artMuseum.name: The peace symbol in the pinwheels
artMuseum.highlight: The colorful pinwheels, Peace Bell and Freedom Bridge, together with the Imjin River fields, form Imjingak's identity system: instantly readable as belonging to Paju, to peace, and to a gentle, restrained memorial aesthetics. From light pinwheels to heavy history, this contrast makes it one of Paju's most memorable cultural images.
points: Imagery: pinwheels, Peace Bell, Freedom Bridge form strong recognition.
Status: Paju's most photogenic peace landmark.
Narrative: it translates the memorial theme into a publicly felt aesthetic experience.

outdoorMuseum.title: Photo & Spotting Guide: Imjingak's Photo Spots
outdoorMuseum.subtitle: As Paju's most recognizable peace landmark, a few structured spots and times greatly improve your photos' usefulness and beauty.

items.name: Wind Hill · pinwheel sea
items.background: From dawn to dusk, thousands of colorful pinwheels turn by the Imjin River — Imjingak's classic 'pinwheel – field' composition, with pinwheel silhouettes especially moving in backlight.
points: Use the pinwheel field as a lead-in to the horizon.
Crouch low to layer pinwheels and field for a steadier frame.

items.name: Freedom Bridge · history overview
items.background: Shoot the memorial bridge from below or from the plaza to frame 'bridge + field + Imjin River' together — Imjingak's most recognizable spot.
points: Side-light at dawn makes the bridge structure softest.
Mind your feet and rails; don't enter closed zones for a frame.

items.name: Peace Bell & dusk
items.background: Blue hour (20–30 min after sunset) balances sky and lawn best — the strongest mood window, the bell solemn against the cool sky.
points: Shoot at blue hour for the most balanced sky and lawn.
Silhouette the Peace Bell leaving a large warm field.

items.name: Imjin River night lights
items.background: At night the park lights up and the Imjin River mirrors the river of lights — good for a closing long-exposure night shot.
points: Use the riverbank as a lead-in to the distance.
Small aperture + tripod for longer exposure.

education.title: Why is this Paju's peace landmark?
education.subtitle: What's most worth learning about Imjingak is not 'it got pretty', but how a stretch of Imjin River field was brought back into public memory while keeping reverence for history.

points.title: A memorial field still used by citizens
points.text: Imjingak is not a 'hide the border and done' case, but a sample that continuously activates the memorial through public-space design and turns it into a shared place.
items: Citizens entrust daily strolls and peace wishes to this field.
Public green and city life coexist long-term.

points.title: Writing memorial ethics into visitor behavior
points.text: The signs, Peace Bell and guide system are not just guides, but let every visitor, while using the space, take part in respect for history and others.
items: Visitors are guided to stay on low-impact paths.
Memorial experience and ecology are not sacrificed to each other.

points.title: Turning border memory into public aesthetics
points.text: Imjingak did not erase the division's past, but through pinwheels, the Peace Bell and signs lets the public, while visiting, realize what this land has gone through.
items: Story depth and memorial experience complete at the same place.
This is exactly the value a non-profit science site should amplify.

audience.title: Who Should Come? From Segment Guide to Custom Itinerary
audience.subtitle: Not just 'you'll like it', but directly telling you how to go, where to start, and which Paju nodes to link.

items.resonance: Free, open, safe flat lawn; kids can chase pinwheels and view the field, and easily walk to Freedom Bridge from the gentle side.
items.tip: Save energy for the high-point stay rather than spending it on steep slopes.

items.resonance: Dawn and dusk colorful pinwheels are Paju's most romantic frames, very photogenic.
items.tip: Count arrival, return and light into your plan so the frame doesn't lose to on-site pace.

items.resonance: As a border memorial sample, separated-family memory, Freedom Bridge and the Peace Bell are worth a close look.
items.tip: Avoid the most crowded weekends; choose dawn or weekday afternoons to truly observe historical details.

items.resonance: Without going far to the countryside, you can observe the DMZ, peace memory and public green near Paju, and link trains, buses and local food — an ideal starting point to understand division and peace.
items.tip: If you can only pick one Paju landmark, Imjingak is the best first stop for the 'peace and memory' theme.

lodging.title: Lodging Guide: Stay close, or stay convenient
lodging.subtitle: Paju is a Gyeonggi town where 'green space and city connect'. We don't recommend specific hotels, but help you analyze two lodging patterns to choose what suits you.

options.name: Imjingak / Munsan area
options.highlight: Staying at Imjingak or Munsan, you walk or take a short ride to Wind Hill, the Peace Bell and the Peace Gondola; the night field and river breeze suit travelers. Best for those focused on 'pinwheel stroll + memorial' with high convenience needs.
options.commute: Commute: about 10–20 min walk to the park, about 5–10 min to the Peace Gondola. Low demand on legs, good for early-morning park walks.
points: About 10–20 min walk to Wind Hill, least effort.
Very rich park night views and dining.
More choices, usually better value.

options.name: Paju city / Heyri Art Village
options.highlight: Staying in Paju city or Heyri Art Village, next to the bus station, art districts and food streets, best for those focused on 'exhibitions + transfer', then take bus / taxi to Imjingak by day.
options.commute: Commute: about 20–30 min by city bus or taxi to Imjingak. Good for drivers or independent travelers wanting absolute convenience.
points: Bus station and art village at hand, good for transfers.
Art exhibitions, cafes and night markets are very rich.
About 20–40 min walk or a short ride to Imjingak.

lodging.peak: Paju's spring cherry-blossom and holiday periods, plus DMZ tourist peak, tighten rooms and raise prices noticeably. Book several weeks ahead; if booking near a holiday, widen the range to Seoul, Goyang and other surrounding areas, then take the train.
tips: Want pinwheel shots: prefer Imjingak / Munsan, walk to the park at dawn.
Value food & art: choose Paju city / Heyri Art Village, walking and dining at hand.
Before booking, confirm breakfast, parking and EV chargers (drivers especially).
Book ahead in peak season and holidays to avoid no rooms or high prices.
